svg.title: How the product-moment mark correlation function describes numerical point marks
svg.desc: A three-step diagram explaining the product-moment mark correlation function for positive numerical marks. The same marked point pattern is used in the first two panels. Pairs with separations near distance r are binned or smoothly weighted, their mark products are averaged, and the result is divided by the squared overall mean mark. Under independent random marking the reference value is one. An illustrative simulation envelope shows sampling variation. Values above or below the envelope indicate unusually large or small pairwise mark products at those distances, not generic mark similarity.
svg.text: How the mark correlation function works

text.tspan: This product-moment function asks whether point pairs at a given distance
have unusually large or small products of positive numerical marks.

svg.text: Attach a mark to each point

text.tspan: A mark is a measured numerical attribute,
such as size, age, biomass, or intensity.
Size and color redundantly encode the
same positive numerical mark.
Bin or smoothly weight pairs whose separation
is near r, then compare their mark products.
Average pair products near r, then divide
by μ², the squared overall mean mark.
Under independent random marking, the
product-moment reference is kₘₘ(r) = 1.
Above envelope → unusually large
pairwise mark products at those distances
Below envelope → unusually small
pairwise mark products at those distances
k
mm
(r) = E[mᵢmⱼ | dᵢⱼ ≈ r] / μ²

svg.text: μ is the overall mean mark. This function measures product magnitude, not generic similarity.

text.tspan: Interpret departures at the distances where they occur.
Use edge correction and simulation envelopes.
For similarity, consider a mark variogram.
